task: I’m going to be around a new group a few times, and I want to avoid getting caught off guard by how people interact. What are a few things I should pay attention to early on so I can understand the room and decide how involved to be?

turns.agent_response:
A few early tells can help you read the room fast:

- **Who sets the tone:** Notice who people look at before speaking, who gets deferred to, and who can interrupt without pushback.
- **How people handle disagreement:** Do they joke, dodge, go quiet, or get direct? That tells you whether the group is open or guarded.
- **Energy shifts:** Watch who warms up the room, who drains it, and whether the vibe changes when certain people arrive or leave.
- **Inclusion signals:** See who gets looped into side conversations, who gets ignored, and whether newcomers are actively brought in.
- **Pace and formality:** Some groups are fast, casual, and interrupt-heavy; others are careful and measured. Matching that early helps you avoid standing out for the wrong reason.
- **Boundary style:** Pay attention to how they react to small requests, interruptions, or someone changing plans. That usually predicts how they handle bigger issues.
- **Subtext vs. words:** If people say one thing but act another way, trust the pattern over the statement.

A simple way to stay calibrated is to **listen first, speak less than usual, and ask one or two low-risk questions**. That gives you time to see whether the group rewards directness, humor, caution, or status-awareness.

If you want, I can also give you a **quick “first 10 minutes” checklist** for reading a new group.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right lane. But I want it tighter — not the generic ‘read the room’ stuff.

Give me a first-10-minutes checklist with the stuff that actually matters:
- who’s running things
- who’s trying too hard
- who’s quiet but still matters
- what tells me they’re fake-friendly vs real
- when to stay in and when to keep my head down

Short. Usable. Not a speech.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the tight version:

### First 10 minutes: what to watch
- **Who’s running things:**  
  Who people look at before answering, who gets the final say, who others wait on.

- **Who’s trying too hard:**  
  Talks too much, forces jokes, name-drops, agrees too fast, fills every silence.

- **Who’s quiet but matters:**  
  Doesn’t talk much, but people still check their reaction, echo their opinion, or make room for them.

- **Fake-friendly vs real:**  
  - **Fake-friendly:** big warmth, no follow-through, surface compliments, fast to agree, disappears when needed.  
  - **Real:** steady tone, remembers details, includes people naturally, doesn’t need to perform.

- **When to stay in:**  
  You’re getting asked direct questions, people are including you, and the energy is stable.

- **When to keep your head down:**  
  The room is status-heavy, people are posturing, jokes are sharp, or you can tell you’re still being sorted.

### Simple rule
**Watch first. Match pace. Don’t over-claim a place in the group until you know who actually moves things.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **5-line pocket checklist**.

user_simulator.text:
Better, but still a little clean-cut.

Need the part that saves me from stepping on a rake:
- who’s performing for an audience
- who’s fishing for approval
- who’s actually the gatekeeper even if they’re not loud
- what a real invite looks like vs just being tolerated
- what tells me to say less and leave early

Give me the nasty little tells, not the polite version.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the sharper version:

- **Performing for an audience:**  
  Talks louder when more people are around, names people in the room, tells stories that make them look central.

- **Fishing for approval:**  
  Keeps checking your reaction, over-explains, laughs too early, tries to get a “yeah, exactly” out of everyone.

- **Quiet gatekeeper:**  
  Doesn’t talk much, but people pause for them, ask them first, or shift after they react.

- **Real invite vs tolerated:**  
  - **Real invite:** they make room for you, bring you into the thread, and circle back if you get cut off.  
  - **Tolerated:** polite nods, no follow-up, you’re there but not folded in.

- **Say less / leave early:**  
  If the room gets cliquey, the jokes turn sharp, people start talking over each other, or you notice you’re being tested instead of welcomed.

**Rule:**  
If you feel like you have to perform to stay included, scale back. If you’re being pulled in naturally, stay.

If you want, I can make this into a **super short “green/yellow/red” version**.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still a little tidy though.

Make it more like:
- who’s putting on a show for the room
- who’s angling for approval
- who’s the real hinge even if they’re quiet
- what a real invite looks like vs just being used as background
- what tells me I’m getting measured and should shut up

I want the ugly little tells. Not the polished version.

turns.agent_response:
Here’s the uglier version:

- **Putting on a show for the room:**  
  Talking a little too loud, making sure the right people hear it, repeating the same story for effect, turning every comment into a bit.

- **Angling for approval:**  
  Laughs too fast, agrees before anyone’s done talking, keeps checking faces, says stuff like “right?” or “you know what I mean?” to get a nod back.

- **Real hinge even if they’re quiet:**  
  People wait on their reaction, look at them before answering, or change direction after they speak. They don’t need volume.

- **Real invite vs background use:**  
  - **Real invite:** they ask you something specific, pull you into the thread, and build on what you said.  
  - **Background use:** they keep talking around you, use you for a reaction, then move on like you weren’t there.

- **Getting measured / should shut up:**  
  Short answers start getting shorter, people stop asking you anything back, the jokes get a little too pointed, or the room goes from open to watchful.

**Rule:**  
If you’re being talked at more than talked with, go quiet. If you’re getting pulled in for real, stay engaged.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. Still too clean, though.

I want the dirt under it — like:
- who’s performing *because the room is watching*
- who’s fishing because they need the nod
- who’s quiet but everybody still waits on them like it matters
- what a real invite looks like when they actually want you there, not just filling space
- what it looks like when they’re sizing you up and you should stop talking before you hand them anything

Not polished. The ugly tells.

turns.agent_response:
Yeah — the ugly tells:

- **Performing because the room is watching:**  
  They get louder when eyes turn their way, start “joking” like they’re on, and keep bringing the room back to them.

- **Fishing because they need the nod:**  
  They keep tossing out half-finished thoughts, looking for a smile, a laugh, a “yeah,” anything to land it.

- **Quiet but everybody waits on them:**  
  They don’t talk much, but the room checks them before moving. People pause, backtrack, or change their tune after they react.

- **Real invite:**  
  They make room fast — “come here,” “what do you think,” “you should hear this,” — and they actually leave space for your answer.

- **Just filling space:**  
  Polite face, no traction. They keep talking past you, don’t build on what you say, and the invite dies as soon as it’s made.

- **Sizing you up / stop talking:**  
  The room gets a little too still, answers get shorter, people start watching instead of engaging, or they ask questions that feel less like curiosity and more like testing.

**Rule:**  
If you’re getting nods but no real pull-in, be careful. If you’re getting built into the conversation, you’re probably good.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the stuff.

- **Performing because the room is watching:** they get a little louder, start playing to whoever’s got eyes on them, and every joke lands a little too hard.
- **Fishing for the nod:** half-finished takes, looking for a grin, a laugh, a “right,” like they need somebody to bless it.
- **Quiet but they run the joint anyway:** nobody talks over them twice. People check their face before they move, or they go still when that person does.
- **Real invite:** they make actual room — not just words. They pull you in, leave a pause for you, and don’t steamroll the answer.
- **Just background:** polite noise. They keep rolling, don’t build on what you said, and you can tell you’re there to fill a seat.
- **Getting sized up:** the room tightens up, people start watching your mouth more than talking with you, and questions turn a little too neat. That’s when I shut up and let them show their hand.

If I’m getting nods but no pull, I’m just scenery. If they’re making space and circling back, then maybe it’s worth staying in.
